coredata.dc:title: A paper-based ratiometric sensor for mercury (II) detection using fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xiaowei
Li, Liangli
Wang, Leyi
Yang, Maohua
Wang, Yu
Zhang, Mei
Jia, Mingyan
coredata.dc:description: Mercury is a highly toxic environmental contaminant known for its significant bioaccumulation, making the rapid and sensitive detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) essential for environmental and public health. In this study, we developed a multicolor nanosensor utilizing dual-emissive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for the swift detection of Hg2+. The sensor comprises yellow-emissive o-phenylenediamine-synthesized CDs (OPD-CDs) and cyan-emissive m-phenylenediamine-synthesized CDs (MPD-CDs), synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method. Comprehensive structural and physicochemical characterization of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs was performed utilizing transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Upon exposure to Hg2+, the fluorescence of OPD-CDs is significantly quenched due to electron transfer, while MPD-CDs maintain their emission, resulting in a color change from yellow to cyan. We thoroughly investigated the luminescence and sensing mechanisms of OPD-CDs and developed a ratiometric detection method that exhibited excellent selectivity, a low limit of detection at 26.8 nM, and high accuracy in real sample analyses, with apparent recovery rates between 99.5 % and 107.5 %. Additionally, we fabricated a portable paper-based device that employs smartphone technology for on-site visual detection of Hg2+, featuring a control zone for signal calibration to mitigate interference from external conditions, demonstrating its potential for accurate on-site detection of Hg2+ in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mercury ions
Multicolor sensing
Paper-based
Rapid detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Hg2+ in real samples
Fabrication and application of a paper-based device
Synthesis and characterization of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs
Optical properties and response to Hg2+ of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs
Optimization of detection conditions and multicolor sensing of Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
Rapid on-site detection based on a paper-based device

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82374000, 82174273); Xinglin Scholar Research Promotion Project of Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine (QJRC2021009); Sichuan Province Science and Technology Activities Funding for Returned Overseas Scholars.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and size distribution images of (A) OPD-CDs and (B) MPD-CDs; XPS full spectrum images of (C) OPD-CDs and (D) MPD-CDs; FT-IR images of (E) OPD-CDs and (F) MPD-CDs.

(A) Response of OPD-CDs to Hg2+ (0–100 μM); (B) Response of MPD-CDs to 100 μM Hg2+; (C) Comparison of fluorescence spectra of 2,3-DAPN and OPD-CDs (Ex = 420 nm); (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of OPD-CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+; (E) Fluorescence lifetimes of OPD-CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+; (F) Zeta potentials of OPD-CDs, Hg2+ and OPD-CDs + Hg2+.

(A) Images and fluorescence spectra of CDs composite solutions at different ratios of OPD-CDs to MPD-CDs; (B) Emission spectra of CDs composite scanned at different excitation wavelengths; (C) Emission intensity at 565 nm of the nanosensor in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (100 μM) under pH 3–7.5 (Ex = 420 nm); (D) Changes of fluorescence spectra of the nanosensor upon the addition of Hg2+ (0–45 μM); (E) The linear fitting curve between I565/I490 and Hg2+ concentrations; (inset of E) Images of the nanosensor upon the addition of Hg2+ (0–100 μM) under a 365 nm UV lamp; (F) CIE chromaticity diagram of the nanosensor upon the addition of Hg2+ (0–100 μM).
Fluorescence response of the nanosensor towards (A) 100 μM and (B) 1 mM of different ions in the presence/absence of 100 μM of Hg2+.

(A) Schematic diagram of the paper-based device; (B) A schematic representation of the on-site detection of Hg2+ utilizing the paper-based device; (C) Images of the paper-based devices upon the addition of increasing concentrations of Hg2+; (D) Linear fitting curve between ΔG/ΔB and Hg2+ concentrations (10–100 μM).
The schematic representation of the quenching of OPD-CDs fluorescence by Hg2+.
Determination of Hg2+ residues in real samples.

head.ce:title: A paper-based ratiometric sensor for mercury (II) detection using fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources, School of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu, 611137, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources
Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources, School of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu, 611137, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian, 6116023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian, 6116023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury is a highly toxic environmental contaminant known for its significant bioaccumulation, making the rapid and sensitive detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) essential for environmental and public health. In this study, we developed a multicolor nanosensor utilizing dual-emissive fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for the swift detection of Hg2+. The sensor comprises yellow-emissive o-phenylenediamine-synthesized CDs (OPD-CDs) and cyan-emissive m-phenylenediamine-synthesized CDs (MPD-CDs), synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method. Comprehensive structural and physicochemical characterization of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs was performed utilizing transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Upon exposure to Hg2+, the fluorescence of OPD-CDs is significantly quenched due to electron transfer, while MPD-CDs maintain their emission, resulting in a color change from yellow to cyan. We thoroughly investigated the luminescence and sensing mechanisms of OPD-CDs and developed a ratiometric detection method that exhibited excellent selectivity, a low limit of detection at 26.8 nM, and high accuracy in real sample analyses, with apparent recovery rates between 99.5 % and 107.5 %. Additionally, we fabricated a portable paper-based device that employs smartphone technology for on-site visual detection of Hg2+, featuring a control zone for signal calibration to mitigate interference from external conditions, demonstrating its potential for accurate on-site detection of Hg2+ in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury, particularly in its ionic form Hg2+, poses significant environmental and health risks due to its high toxicity and persistence [1,2]. This heavy metal accumulates in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems, leading to detrimental effects on flora and fauna. As mercury enters the food chain, it ultimately impacts human health, causing neurological, renal, and cardiovascular damage, which can be fatal [3–6]. Consequently, effective monitoring of Hg2+ is crucial for safeguarding both environmental integrity and public health.

Various analytical methods have been developed to detect Hg2+, including inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7,8], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [9,10], and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [11]. While these techniques achieve high sensitivity, they universally suffer from bulky instrumentation requirements, prolonged sample pretreatment, and prohibitive operational costs. Notwithstanding the enhancement of portability afforded by electrochemical analysis [12,13], a dependence on specialised equipment remains, susceptibility to redox-active interferents, thus restricting the capacity for on-site detection of mercury ions. In contrast, fluorescent sensors, utilized for the detection of metal ions, have emerged as a promising alternative owing to their simplicity, high sensitivity, and rapid response times. [14–16]. Numerous fluorescent sensing platforms have been explored for Hg2+ detection, utilizing quantum dots [17], organic polymers [18], metal-organic frameworks [19], organic small fluorophores [20], and gold/silver nanoclusters [21].

Recent advancements in carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent sensors have garnered significant attention owing to their advantageous properties such as excellent optical performance, straightforward synthesis, low biotoxicity, affordability, ease of modification, and good solubility in water [22]. While several CD-based sensors for Hg2+ detection have been reported [23–25], many rely solely on single fluorescence intensity measurements. This approach can lead to inaccuracies due to environmental fluctuations. Ratiometric fluorescent sensors present a solution by employing the intensity ratio of fluorescence at two distinct wavelengths to mitigate environmental variability [26]. These sensors also facilitate visible color changes upon analyte exposure, enhancing their utility for visual analysis [27]. In this regard, ratiometric fluorescent sensors can be employed as a multicolor sensing platform for visual analysis. Despite some multicolor nano-sensors for Hg2+ detection being developed [28], the majority involve time-consuming multistep synthesis processes that increase costs.

Paper-based devices containing fluorescent nano-sensors have been extensively utilized in the analysis of environmental pollutants [29] and food safety [30] due to their advantages of ease of use, low cost, and portability. Visual detection in the field could be achieved using an ultraviolet lamp as an excitation light source. Moreover, the widespread use of smartphone is facilitating the development of portable analytical device. Images captured by the smartphone camera could be processed directly via a smartphone application.

In this study, we introduce a novel multicolor nanosensor based on dual-emissive fluorescent CDs for the rapid detection of Hg2+. Our approach utilizes yellow-emissive o-phenylenediamine-synthesized CDs (OPD-CDs) and cyan-emissive m-phenylenediamine-synthesized CDs (MPD-CDs), synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method. Upon exposure to Hg2+ ions, the OPD-CDs fluorescence is quenched due to electron transfer (Scheme 1 ), while the MPD-CD fluorescence remains stable. Consequently, the sensor color transitions from yellow to cyan. We propose a ratiometric determination method that demonstrates exceptional selectivity and accuracy in real samples such as leech, seaweed, and honeysuckle. Furthermore, we developed a portable paper-based device integrated with smartphone technology for on-site visual detection of Hg2+. This device features a calibration control zone to account for external variable interferences, thereby enhancing the reliability of detection results. In comparison to other sensors for Hg2+ (Table S1), Our nanosensor not only achieves low detection limits and good selectivity but also supports multicolor sensing with self-calibration capabilities.

O-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, anhydrous ethanol, mercuric chloride, and other metal salts are procured from commercial suppliers and do not necessitate additional processing. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) is obtained from a microporous water purification system (Ulupure, China). The citrate buffer solution was prepared by dissolving 0.1 M citric acid and 0.2 M disodium hydrogen phosphate in deionized water. All experiments were conducted in the aforementioned buffer solution.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured with a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Persee, China). Fluorescence spectra were detected using an F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang Technology, China). A WFH-203B triple-wavelength UV analyzer (Shanghai Chitang Industry, China) was employed for ultraviolet irradiation. The pH was determined by utilizing a PHS-3C pH meter (Leici, China). The morphological characterization and particle size distribution of the OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs were obtained through a Talos F200S transmission electron microscope (TEM) and an Anton Paar Litesizer™ Particle Analyzer. The elemental functional groups of the OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs were characterized utilizing an ESCALAB Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and a NICOLET iS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The high-resolution mass spectrum (HR-MS) of OPD-CDs was determined using a Q Exactive Orbitrap mass spectrometry (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Fluorescence lifetimes were determined by utilizing an Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrometer. Zeta potentials were detected by the Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The Agilent 7850 inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) was employed to detect the residual Hg2+ concentration in leech, seaweed and honeysuckle.

43.25 mg of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol. The solution was subsequently transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling down the resulting mixture was centrifuged for 10 min (10,000 rpm), and then the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The fluorescent OPD-CDs were obtained after the filtrate was purified in a 1000 kDa MWCO dialysis kit for 6 h. OPD-CDs were vacuum-dried and stored at 4 °C for further use. To prepare MPD-CDs, 43.25 mg of m-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulting mixture was then subjected to the same procedure as the OPD-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of 0.15 mg/mL of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs composites solutions in different ratios (1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 5:1, 6:1, 7:1, 8:1, 9:1, 10:1) were measured. The optimal ratio of the composites was determined by comparing their fluorescence spectra and by observing their color under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. The optimal pH for detection was also determined from the fluorescence spectra of CDs composite (8:1) in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (100 μM) at various pH values (3, 4, 5, 6, 6.5, 7, 7.5). The fluorescence spectra of CDs composite (8:1) incubated with Hg2+ (100 μM) for different time (1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 min) were scanned to get the optimal reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The leech, seaweed, and honeysuckle are purchased from the local market. Hg2+ in these samples was extracted based on previous literature [31]. The samples were pulverized through a pulverizer and passed through a No. 3 sieve to control particle size. Concurrently, all glassware utilized in the processing of the samples was immersed in a nitric acid solution overnight. 10–15 mg of the sample was added to a stoppered conical flask, followed by 15 mL of nitric acid. The mixture was heated in a water bath at 60 °C for 12 h. Once cooled, the samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane. Subsequently, the pH was adjusted to neutral with a 5 M sodium hydroxide solution. Finally, the sample was diluted to 20 mL with citrate buffer to obtain the prepared sample solution for detection. Hg2+ in the solutions was detected based on the nanosensor. At the same time, the spike-recovery experiments were conducted by spiking 20, 40, and 60 μM of Hg2+ to the real sample solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and application of a paper-based device
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the non-fluorescent cellulose filter paper was cut into circular test strips with a diameter of 1 cm. After the sheets were immersed in 0.15 mg/mL of CDs composite solution for 1 min, they were dried naturally to get the test paper. In order to enhance the portability of the test paper, a device was then developed. It consists of three parts: the base, the test paper, and the top cover. The base contains two 1 cm diameter circular recesses, each of which can hold one test strip. The top cover also includes two 0.5 cm diameter circular holes, which align with the corresponding recesses on the base. In the device, one well was used to add the control solution (C), while the other was employed to add the test solution (T). Finally, the test solution and the blank solution were introduced to the device, where they were incubated for 20 min. Images of the device were captured with a smartphone camera (Xiaomi 14, China) under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. The green (G) and blue (B) values of the control and test areas were extracted using a smartphone application, Color Grab. The linear fitting curves were plotted, and the concentration of Hg2+ in the leech, seaweed, and honeysuckle was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs with distinct emission characteristics was achieved through a straightforward one-step solvothermal process, utilizing o-phenylenediamine and m-phenylenediamine as the sole carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The TEM images (Fig. 1 A and B) demonstrate that both OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs are uniformly dispersed spherical particles with an average particle size of 2.19 and 1.18 nm (lower right inset of Fig. 1A and B), consistent with DLS results (Fig. S2), respectively. The HR-TEM maps (upper right inset of Fig. 1A and B) indicate that the lattice spacing of the OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs is 0.275 and 0.222 nm, respectively. The elemental composition and surface functional groups of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs were investigated using XPS and FT-IR. The full XPS spectra (Fig. 1C and D) illustrate that both OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs contain carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N), yet exhibit distinct elemental contents. The atomic percentages of C, O, and N in the OPD-CDs were 67.89 %, 13.37 %, and 18.74 %, respectively, while those of the MPD-CDs were 81.46 %, 14.08 %, and 14.46 %, respectively. The deconvolution of C1s spectrum of OPD-CDs (Fig. S1A) has four characteristic peaks, CC at 284.8 eV, C–O at 285.6 eV, C–N at 286.6 eV, and CO at 288.8 eV. The peaks in the O1s spectrum of OPD-CDs (Fig. S1C) are ascribed to CO at 531.9 eV and C–O at 532.8 eV and that in the N1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) are ascribed to C–N at 398.9 eV, N–H at 399.7 eV, pyrrole N at 400.7 eV, and NO/N–O at 406.3 eV. The deconvolution of the C1s spectra of MPD-CDs (Fig. S1D) revealed the presence of three distinct peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.27 eV, and 288.7 eV, which were attributed to the CC, C–O/C–N, and CO functional groups, respectively. The O1s spectra of MPD-CDs (Fig. S1F) exhibited two peaks at 531.37 eV and 532.76 eV, which were attributed to the CO and C–O, respectively. Additionally, the N1s spectra of MPD-CDs (Fig. S1E) displayed a peak at 399.28 eV, suggesting the potential presence of pyridine N. The FT-IR plots of OPD-CDs (Fig. 1E) at 3060.75, 2675.57, 1620, 1414.74, and 729.93 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of C–H, O–H, CC, C–N, and N–H, respectively. The FT-IR plots of MPD-CDs (Fig. 1F) exhibit peaks at 1273.08 cm−1 and 1088.48 cm−1, which are ascribed to the following vibrations: ν (O–H), ν (C–H), ν (CC)/ν (CO), ν (C–O), ν (C–N) [32]. Using fluorescein with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 0.92 in water as a reference [33], the QY of OPD CDs was determined and calculated to be 3.91 %. The QY of MPD-CDs was determined to be 19.99 %, employing quinine sulfate with a QY of 0.54 in a 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution as a reference [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and response to Hg2+ of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs were investigated by recording their UV–Vis absorption spectra and steady-state fluorescence spectra. The UV–Vis spectra of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs exhibited significant absorption at 420 and 400 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). The maximum emission peaks of OPD-CDs (Fig. S4A) and MPD-CDs (Fig. S4B) are 490 nm and 565 nm at varying excitation wavelengths, respectively. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, OPD-CDs emitted yellow fluorescence, while MPD-CDs emitted bright cyan fluorescence (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence of OPD-CDs was gradually quenched by adding 0–100 μM Hg2+. In comparison, the fluorescence intensity of MPD-CDs was almost unaffected by adding 100 μM of Hg2+ (Fig. 2B). These results demonstrate that the combination of OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs has the potential to facilitate ratiometric detection of Hg2+ in a multicolor manner.

Despite the complexity of the fluorescence mechanism of CDs, previous research indicates that the fluorescence of OPD-CDs may originate from the formation of small molecule fluorophores [35]. HR-MS was employed to conduct a comprehensive analysis of the structure of OPD-CDs. The mass spectrum of OPD-CDs (Fig. S6) revealed the presence of fragments of 2,3-diaminophenazine (2,3-DAPN). The fluorescence spectrum of 2,3-DAPN was further measured, which demonstrated a luminescent nature consistent with that of OPD-CDs (Fig. 2C). These results suggest that the emission of OPD-CDs may originate from 2,3-DAPN, which is formed by the dimerization of o-phenylenediamine during the solvothermal reaction.

To gain further insight into the quenching mechanism of Hg2+ on the fluorescence of OPD-CDs, the UV–Vis absorption spectra were initially scanned. The absorption band of OPD-CDs remained unaltered following the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. 2D), suggesting that Hg2+ may quench the fluorescence of OPD-CDs through an electron transfer between the ligand on the surface of OPD-CDs and Hg2+ [36]. To validate the hypothesis, fluorescence lifetimes and zeta potentials of OPD-CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ were subsequently investigated. The presence of Hg2+ effectively shortened the fluorescence lifetime of OPD-CDs from 1.915 to 1.377 ns (Fig. 2E), indicating that a dynamic quenching process occurred and that notable interactions between OPD-CDs and Hg2+ existed [37]. The zeta potentials of OPD-CDs and Hg2+ are −13.4 mV and 31.2 mV, respectively, and that of their combination is 28.2 mV (Fig. 2F). These experimental results confirmed that the fluorescence of OPD-CDs was quenched by Hg2+ through electron transfer. It is notable that, despite the fact that the small molecule 2,3-DAPN is also responsive to Hg2+, its poor water solubility and low selectivity restrict its applicability in Hg2+ detection (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions and multicolor sensing of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: A multicolor nanosensor for ratiometric Hg2+ detection was developed based on an OPD-CDs and MPD-CDs composite. We first optimized the detection conditions, including the ratio of OPD-CDs to MPD-CDs, the excitation wavelength, pH, and the response time. In order for the nanosensor to exhibit a discernible color response to Hg2+, the nanosensor itself should be predominantly yellow. The visualization effects and fluorescence spectra of composite solutions at different ratios of OPD-CDs to MPD-CDs (1:1 to 10:1) were examined. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, the color of solution changes from cyan to yellow as the ratio increases, and yellow is the dominant color when the ratio reaches 8:1. Concurrently, the maximum emission peaks of composites undergo a shift from 490 nm to 565 nm as the ratio increases. Thus, CDs composite at a ratio of OPD-CDs to MPD-CDs 8:1 was used as the multicolor nanosensor for Hg2+ detection. In order to enhance the sensitivity of the ratiometric detection, it is necessary to ensure that the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor at 565 nm is greater than that at 490 nm. The excitation wavelength was evaluated because it can influence the emission intensity of the CDs composite at the two wavelengths. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the emission intensity at 565 nm is greater than that at 490 nm under excitation at 420 nm. Fig. 3C displays the emission intensity at 565 nm of the nanosensor in the absence and presence of Hg2+ under varying pH. At a pH of 7.0, the Hg2+ quench effect on the CDs composite was most pronounced. The response time was also evaluated. The fluorescence intensity at 565 nm reached a stable level 20 min after the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S8). According to these results, the optimal ratio of OPD-CDs to MPD-CDs, excitation wavelength, pH, and response time of the nanosensor are 8:1, 420 nm, 7.0, and 20 min, respectively.

Under the optimal detection conditions, the fluorescence titration experiment was conducted. As the concentration of Hg2+ increased, the fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor at 565 nm exhibited a gradual decrease, while that at 490 nm remained unaltered (Fig. 3D). An excellent linear relationship was observed between I565/I490 and the concentration of Hg2+ from 0 to 40 μM, with an R2 value of 0.991 (Fig. 3E). Furthermore, the detection limit was calculated to be 26.8 nM based on the triple standard deviation of the blank solution. The change in fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor upon exposure to Hg2+ was in agreement with the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) chromaticity diagram. (Fig. 3F). The gradual color transition from yellow to cyan of the nanosensor upon exposure to Hg2+ was observed under a UV lamp at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 3E).

Selectivity is a crucial parameter to evaluate the performance of a fluorescent sensor. The selectivity of the nanosensor for Hg2+ and other interfering ions was investigated, including cations of Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, K+, Li+, Mn2+, Na+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ag+ (NaCl masking), and Zn2+, as well as the anions of I−, NO3 −, F−, HCO3 −, HSO3 −, NO2 −, Br−, CO3 −, SO4 −, and Cl−. It was observed that only Hg2+ significantly reduced the intensity of the nanosensor, whereas the other ions had no discernible effect on the intensity of the nanosensor (Fig. 4 A). Even when the interfering ions concentration was ten times that of Hg2+, the interfering ions exhibited minimal impact on the detection of Hg2+ (Fig. 4B). The excellent selectivity of the nanosensor provides a robust basis for the detection of Hg2+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the nanosensor in detecting Hg2+ in real samples was investigated. Leech, seaweed, and honeysuckle were pre-treated, and the obtained sample solutions were detected. As illustrated in Table 1 , the concentrations of Hg2+ residues in leech, seaweed, and honeysuckle samples were 3.37 μM, 1.07 μM, and 0.73 μM, respectively. Spike-recovery experiments were conducted to assess the nanosensor-based method's accuracy. The apparent recoveries were within the range of 99.6 %–107.7 %, with small relative standard deviations (RSD≤ 0.63 %), suggesting the good accuracy of our proposed method. To further verify the reliability of the results, ICP-MS was utilized to detect Hg2+ in the above sample solutions. The results of both methodologies were found to be similar, thereby further substantiating the considerable potential of the nanosensor to accurately detect Hg2+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid on-site detection based on a paper-based device
ce:section.ce:para: In order to facilitate the more convenient and intuitive detection of Hg2+, we developed a test paper-based rapid detection device for Hg2+. The CDs composite was immobilized on the non-fluorescent filter paper pieces to get the test strip. A test paper-based device was then developed, as displayed in Fig. 5 A. In addition to the test area (T) for the sample, a control area (C) for the blank was incorporated into the device to exclude interference from variable external conditions by calibrating the signal of the test area. The RGB data of the test papers in the device were extracted using a smartphone application, Color Grab, in a 365 nm UV light box (Fig. 5B). As illustrated in Fig. 5C, the visual observation of the test area reveals a clear shift from green to blue as the concentration of Hg2+ increases. The B and G values of each test zone were subtracted from those of the corresponding blank zone, resulting in the calibrated values, ΔB and ΔG. A significant linear relationship was observed between ΔB/ΔG and Hg2+ concentrations, with an R2 value of 0.994 (Fig. 5D). The limit of detection of the device was calculated to be 1.90 μM based on the equation LOD = 3σ/S (n = 20). In contrast, the R2 value of the linear fitting curve between uncalibrated B/G and Hg2+ concentrations was 0.974 (Fig. S9). To determine the feasibility of the paper-based rapid test device in real samples, spike-recovery experiments were conducted in leech, seaweed, and honeysuckle. As listed in Table S2, the apparent recoveries ranged from 98.5 % to 103.9 %, with RSDs of ≤1.88 %. These results indicated that the paper-based device can achieve the rapid on-site detection of Hg2+ with good accuracy and reliability.

In conclusion, we synthesized yellow-emissive OPD-CDs and blue-emissive MPD-CDs via a one-step solvothermal method and used their composite to develop a multicolor nanosensor for the rapid visual detection of Hg2+. The fluorescence of OPD-CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ through electron transfer, resulting in a color change from yellow to cyan of the nanosensor. A novel approach to ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ was developed and demonstrated excellent selectivity towards Hg2+ and a low detection limit (26.8 nM). This approach has been successfully applied for detecting Hg2+ in leech, honeysuckle, and seaweed, and the results were in good agreement with those obtained by ICP-MS. Furthermore, we constructed a paper-based device with a smartphone readout for on-site visual detection of Hg2+ and tested its efficacy in real samples, achieving satisfactory accuracy. This work demonstrates the great potential of CDs in developing a multicolor sensing platform for the rapid visual on-site monitoring of hazardous substances.

Xiaowei Guo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liangli Li: Resources. Leyi Wang: Resources. Maohua Yang: Resources. Yu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Mei Zhang: Funding acquisition. Mingyan Jia: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82374000, 82174273); Xinglin Scholar Research Promotion Project of Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine (QJRC2021009); Sichuan Province Science and Technology Activities Funding for Returned Overseas Scholars.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2025.128036.
